suboptimal_prediction = true_conditional_means .+ 0.1  # Perturb optimal prediction
suboptimal_mse = mse_from_constant_prediction(X, suboptimal_prediction, partition_points)

println("MSE with optimal prediction: ", round(optimal_mse, digits=4))
println("MSE with suboptimal prediction: ", round(suboptimal_mse, digits=4))

7.6 8.6 Regular Conditional Distributions

While conditional expectation handles scalar-valued random variables, many applications require conditioning entire distributions. Regular conditional distributions provide this extension when the underlying space has suitable structure.

Definition 8.7 (Regular Conditional Distribution). Let X: Ω → ℝᵈ be a random vector and 𝒢 ⊆ ℱ be a sub-σ-algebra. A family of probability measures {P(·|𝒢)(ω) : ω ∈ Ω} on (ℝᵈ, ℬ(ℝᵈ)) is called a regular conditional distribution of X given 𝒢 if:

  1. For each B ∈ ℬ(ℝᵈ), ω ↦ P(X ∈ B | 𝒢)(ω) is 𝒢-measurable
  2. For P-almost all ω, P(·|𝒢)(ω) is a probability measure
  3. For all B ∈ ℬ(ℝᵈ) and A ∈ 𝒢: ∫_A P(X ∈ B | 𝒢)(ω) P(dω) = P(X ∈ B, A)

Theorem 8.8 (Existence of Regular Conditional Distributions). Let X: Ω → ℝᵈ and Y: Ω → ℝᵏ be random vectors. Then there exists a regular conditional distribution P(X ∈ · | Y) such that for any bounded measurable function f: ℝᵈ → ℝ:

E[f(X) | Y] = ∫ f(x) P(X ∈ dx | Y) (a.s.)

The proof requires advanced measure theory beyond our scope, but the result enables rigorous treatment of conditional distributions in applied settings.

7.6.1 Application to Bayesian Inference

In Bayesian statistics, if θ represents parameters and X represents data, then the posterior distribution satisfies:

P(θ ∈ · | X) = π(· | X)

where π(· | X) is the regular conditional distribution of θ given X. The posterior mean E[θ | X] equals ∫ θ π(dθ | X), connecting Bayesian inference directly to conditional expectation theory.

7.7 8.7 Conditional Independence

Conditional independence extends the notion of independence to conditioning scenarios, proving essential in graphical models and causal inference.

Definition 8.9 (Conditional Independence). Random vectors X and Y are conditionally independent given σ-algebra 𝒢, written X ⫫ Y | 𝒢, if for all bounded measurable functions f and g:

E[f(X)g(Y) | 𝒢] = E[f(X) | 𝒢] E[g(Y) | 𝒢] (a.s.)

When regular conditional distributions exist, this is equivalent to: P(X ∈ A, Y ∈ B | 𝒢) = P(X ∈ A | 𝒢) P(Y ∈ B | 𝒢) (a.s.)

for all Borel sets A, B.

Example 8.2 (Markov Property). A stochastic process {Xₜ}ₜ≥₀ has the Markov property if for all s < t:

X_t ⫫ ℱₛ | Xₛ

where ℱₛ = σ(Xᵤ : u ≤ s) represents the history up to time s. This states that given the present state Xₛ, the future X_t is independent of the past ℱₛ.

7.7.1 Computational Example: Naive Bayes

using StatsBase, Random
Random.seed!(456)

# Simulate conditional independence in classification
n = 5000
n_features = 3

# Generate class labels
classes = rand(1:3, n)

# Generate features conditionally independent given class
# Class 1: features ~ N(0, 1), Class 2: features ~ N(2, 1), Class 3: features ~ N(-1, 1)
features = zeros(n, n_features)
for i in 1:n
    if classes[i] == 1
        features[i, :] = randn(n_features)
    elseif classes[i] == 2
        features[i, :] = 2 .+ randn(n_features)
    else
        features[i, :] = -1 .+ randn(n_features)
    end
end

# Test conditional independence assumption
function test_conditional_independence(features, classes, class_val)
    mask = classes .== class_val
    if sum(mask) < 50
        return NaN, NaN
    end
    
    class_features = features[mask, :]
    
    # Compute correlation between features 1 and 2 given class
    corr_12 = cor(class_features[:, 1], class_features[:, 2])
    
    # Compute correlation between features 1 and 3 given class  
    corr_13 = cor(class_features[:, 1], class_features[:, 3])
    
    return corr_12, corr_13
end

println("Testing conditional independence assumption:")
for c in 1:3
    corr12, corr13 = test_conditional_independence(features, classes, c)
    if !isnan(corr12)
        println("Class $c: corr(F1,F2|C) = $(round(corr12, digits=3)), corr(F1,F3|C) = $(round(corr13, digits=3))")
    end
end

# Naive Bayes classification using conditional independence
function naive_bayes_predict(train_features, train_classes, test_features)
    n_classes = maximum(train_classes)
    n_test = size(test_features, 1)
    n_features = size(test_features, 2)
    
    predictions = zeros(Int, n_test)
    
    for i in 1:n_test
        log_probs = zeros(n_classes)
        
        for c in 1:n_classes
            class_mask = train_classes .== c
            class_features = train_features[class_mask, :]
            
            if size(class_features, 1) > 0
                # Log prior
                log_probs[c] = log(mean(class_mask))
                
                # Log likelihood under conditional independence
                for f in 1:n_features
                    μ = mean(class_features[:, f])
                    σ = std(class_features[:, f])
                    if σ > 0
                        log_probs[c] += -(test_features[i, f] - μ)^2 / (2 * σ^2) - log(σ)
                    end
                end
            else
                log_probs[c] = -Inf
            end
        end
        
        predictions[i] = argmax(log_probs)
    end
    
    return predictions
end

# Split data for testing
train_idx = 1:div(n, 2)
test_idx = (div(n, 2)+1):n

predictions = naive_bayes_predict(
    features[train_idx, :], classes[train_idx],
    features[test_idx, :]
)

accuracy = mean(predictions .== classes[test_idx])
println("Naive Bayes accuracy: ", round(accuracy, digits=3))

7.8 8.8 Connection to Bayesian Inference

Conditional expectation provides the theoretical foundation for Bayesian inference. In the Bayesian framework, parameters θ and data X are jointly distributed, and inference proceeds by computing the posterior distribution of θ given observed data X = x.

The posterior expectation of any function g(θ) equals: E[g(θ) | X = x] = ∫ g(θ) π(θ | x) dθ

where π(θ | x) is the posterior density. This is precisely the conditional expectation E[g(θ) | σ(X)] evaluated at the observed data.

7.8.1 Posterior Risk and Decision Theory

In decision theory, one chooses action a to minimize posterior expected loss: ρ(a | x) = E[L(θ, a) | X = x]

where L(θ, a) is the loss function. The Bayes rule selects a*(x) = argminₐ ρ(a | x), which by the L² projection property minimizes the overall risk when L is squared error loss.

Example 8.3 (Bayesian Linear Regression). Consider the model Y = Xβ + ε where ε ~ N(0, σ²I), β ~ N(μ₀, Σ₀), and σ² is known. The posterior distribution of β given Y = y satisfies:

β | Y = y ~ N(μₙ, Σₙ)

where: - Σₙ = (Σ₀⁻¹ + X’X/σ²)⁻¹
- μₙ = Σₙ(Σ₀⁻¹μ₀ + X’y/σ²)

The posterior mean μₙ = E[β | Y = y] minimizes the squared error loss E[(β - b)’(β - b) | Y = y] over all vectors b.

using LinearAlgebra, Distributions, Random
Random.seed!(789)

# Bayesian linear regression demonstration
n, p = 100, 3
X = randn(n, p)
true_beta = [1.5, -0.8, 2.1]
sigma = 0.5
y = X * true_beta + sigma * randn(n)

# Prior parameters
mu_0 = zeros(p)
Sigma_0 = 4.0 * I(p)  # Uninformative prior

# Posterior parameters
Sigma_n_inv = inv(Sigma_0) + X' * X / sigma^2
Sigma_n = inv(Sigma_n_inv)
mu_n = Sigma_n * (inv(Sigma_0) * mu_0 + X' * y / sigma^2)

println("True beta: ", round.(true_beta, digits=3))
println("Posterior mean (conditional expectation): ", round.(mu_n, digits=3))
println("Posterior standard deviations: ", round.(sqrt.(diag(Sigma_n)), digits=3))

# Compare with frequentist estimate
beta_hat = (X' * X) \ (X' * y)
println("OLS estimate: ", round.(beta_hat, digits=3))

# Demonstrate that posterior mean minimizes posterior expected squared loss
function posterior_expected_loss(b, mu_n, Sigma_n)
    return tr(Sigma_n) + (b - mu_n)' * (b - mu_n)
end

# Test different estimators
candidates = [mu_n, beta_hat, zeros(p), true_beta]
candidate_names = ["Posterior mean", "OLS", "Zero", "True beta"]

println("\nPosterior expected squared losses:")
for (i, (cand, name)) in enumerate(zip(candidates, candidate_names))
    loss = posterior_expected_loss(cand, mu_n, Sigma_n)
    println("$name: ", round(loss, digits=4))
end

7.9 8.9 Advanced Properties and Extensions

7.9.1 Martingales and Conditional Expectation

A sequence {Xₙ} is a martingale with respect to filtration {ℱₙ} if E[Xₙ₊₁ | ℱₙ] = Xₙ for all n. This definition directly employs conditional expectation and leads to powerful convergence theorems foundational in stochastic analysis.

7.9.2 Conditional Expectation in Infinite Dimensions

When X takes values in a separable Banach space B, conditional expectation E[X | 𝒢] can be defined as the Pettis integral, extending the theory to functional data analysis and stochastic differential equations in infinite dimensions.

7.9.3 Optimal Stopping and Dynamic Programming

The value function in optimal stopping problems satisfies: V(x) = max{g(x), E[V(X₁) | X₀ = x]}

where g is the stopping reward and X₁ is the next state. This Bellman equation fundamentally relies on conditional expectation for its formulation and solution.

7.10 Exercises

7.10.1 Theoretical Exercises

  1. Conditional Variance Formula: Prove that for X, Y ∈ L²(P), Var(X) = E[Var(X | Y)] + Var(E[X | Y]) Interpret this decomposition in terms of explained and unexplained variation.

  2. Conditional Hölder’s Inequality: For p, q > 1 with 1/p + 1/q = 1, X ∈ Lᵖ(P), Y ∈ Lᵍ(P), and sub-σ-algebra 𝒢, prove: |E[XY | 𝒢]| ≤ (E[|X|ᵖ | 𝒢])^(1/p) (E[|Y|ᵍ | 𝒢])^(1/q) (a.s.)

  3. Nested Conditioning: Let ℋ ⊆ 𝒢 ⊆ ℱ be sub-σ-algebras and X ∈ L¹(P). Prove that E[X | ℋ] = E[E[X | 𝒢] | ℋ] (a

8 Chapter 9: Generating Functions, Transforms, and Inequalities

This chapter develops the theory of generating functions and moment generating functions as analytical tools for studying probability distributions. We establish fundamental concentration inequalities that provide exponential tail bounds for sums of independent random variables, with applications to high-dimensional statistics and learning theory.

8.1 9.1 Probability Generating Functions

For discrete random variables taking non-negative integer values, probability generating functions provide a powerful analytical framework for computing moments and studying convergence.

Definition 9.1 (Probability Generating Function). Let X be a random variable taking values in {0, 1, 2, …}. The probability generating function (PGF) of X is defined as

G_X(s) = E[s^X] = ∑_{k=0}^∞ P(X = k) s^k

for all s ∈ ℂ such that the series converges.

The radius of convergence of G_X(s) is at least 1, since G_X(1) = ∑_{k=0}^∞ P(X = k) = 1. For |s| ≤ 1, the series converges absolutely.

Theorem 9.1 (Properties of Probability Generating Functions). Let X be a non-negative integer-valued random variable with PGF G_X(s). Then:

  1. G_X(1) = 1
  2. G_X’(1) = E[X]
  3. G_X’’(1) = E[X(X-1)] = E[X²] - E[X]
  4. More generally, G_X^{(n)}(1) = E[X(X-1)⋯(X-n+1)]

Proof. Properties (1)-(3) follow by differentiating the series term by term and evaluating at s = 1. For property (4), we have

G_X^{(n)}(s) = ∑_{k=n}^∞ k(k-1)⋯(k-n+1) P(X = k) s^{k-n}

Setting s = 1 yields the result. □

The PGF uniquely determines the distribution, as the coefficients can be recovered via P(X = k) = G_X^{(k)}(0)/k!.

8.1.1 Example 9.1: Poisson Distribution

For X ~ Poisson(λ), we have P(X = k) = e^{-λ} λ^k/k!. The PGF is

G_X(s) = ∑_{k=0}^∞ e^{-λ} (λs)^k/k! = e^{-λ} e^{λs} = e^{λ(s-1)}

From this, we immediately obtain E[X] = G_X’(1) = λ and Var(X) = G_X’‘(1) + G_X’(1) - (G_X’(1))² = λ.

using Distributions, Plots

# Compute PGF numerically for Poisson(3) at various points
λ = 3.0
poisson_dist = Poisson(λ)

function pgf_poisson_numerical(s, λ, max_k=50)
    """Numerically compute PGF for Poisson(λ) by truncating the series"""
    return sum(pdf(Poisson(λ), k) * s^k for k in 0:max_k)
end

function pgf_poisson_exact(s, λ)
    """Exact PGF for Poisson distribution"""
    return exp(λ * (s - 1))
end

# Compare numerical and exact computation
s_values = 0.1:0.1:2.0
numerical_values = [pgf_poisson_numerical(s, λ) for s in s_values]
exact_values = [pgf_poisson_exact(s, λ) for s in s_values]

plot(s_values, numerical_values, label="Numerical (truncated)", marker=:circle)
plot!(s_values, exact_values, label="Exact formula", line=:dash, linewidth=2)
xlabel!("s")
ylabel!("G_X(s)")
title!("Probability Generating Function for Poisson(3)")

8.2 9.2 Moment Generating Functions and Laplace Transforms

Moment generating functions extend the concept of generating functions to arbitrary real-valued random variables and provide the foundation for proving limit theorems.

Definition 9.2 (Moment Generating Function). Let X be a random variable. The moment generating function (MGF) of X is defined as

M_X(t) = E[e^{tX}]

for all t ∈ ℝ such that the expectation exists.

The MGF may not exist for all values of t, but when it exists in a neighborhood of 0, it uniquely determines the distribution and generates all moments.

Theorem 9.2 (Moment Generation Property). If M_X(t) exists in an open interval containing 0, then X has finite moments of all orders, and

E[X^n] = M_X^{(n)}(0)

for all n ≥ 1.

Proof. Under the regularity conditions, we can expand e^{tX} in a power series and interchange expectation with differentiation:

M_X(t) = E[∑_{n=0}^∞ (tX)^n/n!] = ∑_{n=0}^∞ E[X^n] t^n/n!

Differentiating n times and setting t = 0 yields the result. □

8.2.1 Laplace Transforms

The moment generating function is closely related to the Laplace transform, which is particularly useful for non-negative random variables.

Definition 9.3 (Laplace Transform). For a random variable X ≥ 0, the Laplace transform is defined as

ℒ_X(s) = E[e^{-sX}]

for s ≥ 0.

Note that ℒ_X(s) = M_X(-s) for s ≥ 0. The Laplace transform always exists for non-negative random variables since e^{-sX} ≤ 1 when s ≥ 0 and X ≥ 0.

8.2.2 Example 9.2: Exponential Distribution

For X ~ Exponential(λ), the density is f(x) = λe^{-λx} for x ≥ 0. The MGF is

M_X(t) = ∫₀^∞ e^{tx} λe^{-λx} dx = λ ∫₀^∞ e^{-(λ-t)x} dx = λ/(λ-t)

for t < λ. From this, E[X] = M_X’(0) = 1/λ and E[X²] = M_X’’(0) = 2/λ², giving Var(X) = 1/λ².

using Distributions, QuadGK

# Verify MGF computation for exponential distribution
function mgf_exponential_numerical(t, λ)
    """Numerically compute MGF for Exp(λ) via integration"""
    if t >= λ
        return Inf
    end
    integrand(x) = exp(t * x) * λ * exp(-λ * x)
    result, _ = quadgk(integrand, 0, 20)  # Integrate to large but finite upper limit
    return result
end

function mgf_exponential_exact(t, λ)
    """Exact MGF for exponential distribution"""
    return t < λ ? λ / (λ - t) : Inf
end

λ = 2.0
t_values = -1:0.1:1.5

numerical_mgf = [mgf_exponential_numerical(t, λ) for t in t_values if t < λ]
exact_mgf = [mgf_exponential_exact(t, λ) for t in t_values if t < λ]
valid_t = [t for t in t_values if t < λ]

plot(valid_t, numerical_mgf, label="Numerical", marker=:circle)
plot!(valid_t, exact_mgf, label="Exact: λ/(λ-t)", line=:dash, linewidth=2)
xlabel!("t")
ylabel!("M_X(t)")
title!("Moment Generating Function for Exp(2)")

8.3 9.3 Chernoff Bounds and the Chernoff Method

The Chernoff bound technique uses moment generating functions to derive exponential tail bounds. This method is fundamental in concentration of measure theory.

Theorem 9.3 (Chernoff Bound). Let X be a random variable with MGF M_X(t). Then for any a > 0,

P(X ≥ a) ≤ inf_{t>0} e^{-ta} M_X(t)

and

P(X ≤ a) ≤ inf_{t<0} e^{-ta} M_X(t)

Proof. For t > 0 and a > 0, we have {X ≥ a} ⊆ {e^{tX} ≥ e^{ta}}. By Markov’s inequality,

P(X ≥ a) = P(e^{tX} ≥ e^{ta}) ≤ E[e^{tX}]/e^{ta} = e^{-ta} M_X(t)

Taking the infimum over t > 0 gives the tightest bound. The second inequality follows similarly. □

The key insight is that we can optimize over the parameter t to obtain the sharpest possible exponential bound.

8.3.1 Example 9.3: Chernoff Bound for Gaussian Random Variables

Let X ~ N(μ, σ²). The MGF is M_X(t) = exp(μt + σ²t²/2). For the upper tail P(X ≥ a) with a > μ, we minimize

g(t) = exp(-ta + μt + σ²t²/2)

Taking the derivative and setting to zero: g’(t) = exp(μt + σ²t²/2 - ta)(-a + μ + σ²t) = 0, which gives t* = (a - μ)/σ². The Chernoff bound becomes

P(X ≥ a) ≤ exp(-(a - μ)²/(2σ²))

This recovers the exact tail bound for Gaussian random variables.

8.4 9.4 Sub-Gaussian Random Variables

Sub-Gaussian random variables generalize Gaussian distributions in terms of tail behavior and provide a rich class for developing concentration inequalities.

Definition 9.4 (Sub-Gaussian Random Variable). A random variable X with E[X] = 0 is sub-Gaussian with parameter σ > 0 if

E[e^{tX}] ≤ e^{σ²t²/2}

for all t ∈ ℝ.

Equivalently, X is sub-Gaussian if its MGF is dominated by that of a Gaussian random variable.

Theorem 9.4 (Sub-Gaussian Tail Bound). If X is sub-Gaussian with parameter σ, then

P(X ≥ t) ≤ e^{-t²/(2σ²)}

for all t ≥ 0.

Proof. Apply the Chernoff bound with the sub-Gaussian MGF condition:

P(X ≥ t) ≤ inf_{λ>0} e^{-λt} E[e^{λX}] ≤ inf_{λ>0} e^{-λt + σ²λ²/2}

Minimizing over λ gives λ* = t/σ², yielding the bound. □

8.4.1 Properties of Sub-Gaussian Random Variables

Sub-Gaussian random variables satisfy several important properties:

  1. Bounded variables: If X is bounded with |X| ≤ K almost surely and E[X] = 0, then X is sub-Gaussian with parameter K.

  2. Gaussian variables: If X ~ N(0, σ²), then X is sub-Gaussian with parameter σ.

  3. Closure under summation: If X₁, …, Xₙ are independent sub-Gaussian with parameters σ₁, …, σₙ, then ∑ᵢ aᵢXᵢ is sub-Gaussian with parameter (∑ᵢ aᵢ²σᵢ²)^{1/2}.

using Distributions, Random
Random.seed!(12345)

# Demonstrate sub-Gaussian property for bounded random variables
function simulate_subgaussian_tail(n_trials=100000)
    # Compare Rademacher (±1 with prob 1/2) to Gaussian tail bounds
    rademacher_samples = rand([-1, 1], n_trials)
    gaussian_samples = randn(n_trials)
    
    # Compute empirical tail probabilities
    t_values = 0.5:0.5:3.0
    
    rademacher_tails = [mean(rademacher_samples .≥ t) for t in t_values]
    gaussian_tails = [mean(gaussian_samples .≥ t) for t in t_values]
    
    # Theoretical bounds (Rademacher has sub-Gaussian parameter 1)
    theoretical_bound = [exp(-t^2/2) for t in t_values]
    
    return t_values, rademacher_tails, gaussian_tails, theoretical_bound
end

t_vals, rad_tails, gauss_tails, theory_bound = simulate_subgaussian_tail()

plot(t_vals, rad_tails, label="Rademacher (empirical)", marker=:circle)
plot!(t_vals, gauss_tails, label="N(0,1) (empirical)", marker=:square)  
plot!(t_vals, theory_bound, label="e^{-t²/2} (theoretical)", 
      line=:dash, linewidth=2)
xlabel!("t")
ylabel!("P(X ≥ t)")
title!("Tail Probabilities for Sub-Gaussian Variables")
yscale!(:log10)

8.5 9.5 Concentration Inequalities

We now develop a comprehensive theory of concentration inequalities that provide exponential bounds on the deviation of sums of independent random variables from their expectation.

8.5.1 Hoeffding’s Inequality

Hoeffding’s inequality is one of the most fundamental concentration results for bounded random variables.

Theorem 9.5 (Hoeffding’s Inequality). Let X₁, …, Xₙ be independent random variables with aᵢ ≤ Xᵢ ≤ bᵢ almost surely. Let S = ∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ Xᵢ and μ = E[S]. Then

P(S - μ ≥ t) ≤ exp(-2t²/∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ(bᵢ - aᵢ)²)

and

P(S - μ ≤ -t) ≤ exp(-2t²/∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ(bᵢ - aᵢ)²)

for all t ≥ 0.

Proof. We prove the upper tail bound using the Chernoff method. For bounded random variables, we first establish a sub-Gaussian MGF bound.

For a zero-mean bounded random variable Z with a ≤ Z ≤ b, we can show that E[e^{tZ}] ≤ e^{t²(b-a)²/8} using the convexity of the exponential function and Jensen’s inequality.

Applying this to Yᵢ = Xᵢ - E[Xᵢ], we get:

P(S - μ ≥ t) = P(∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ Yᵢ ≥ t) ≤ exp(-λt) ∏ᵢ₌₁ⁿ E[e^{λYᵢ}] ≤ exp(-λt + λ²∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ(bᵢ - aᵢ)²/8)

Optimizing over λ gives λ* = 4t/∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ(bᵢ - aᵢ)², yielding the stated bound. □

8.5.2 Bernstein’s Inequality

Bernstein’s inequality provides sharper bounds when the random variables have small variance relative to their range.

Theorem 9.6 (Bernstein’s Inequality). Let X₁, …, Xₙ be independent random variables with E[Xᵢ] = 0, E[Xᵢ²] ≤ σᵢ², and |Xᵢ| ≤ M almost surely. Let S = ∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ Xᵢ and σ² = ∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ σᵢ². Then

P(S ≥ t) ≤ exp(-t²/(2σ² + 2Mt/3))

for all t ≥ 0.

Bernstein’s inequality interpolates between Gaussian behavior (when t ≪ σ²/M) and the exponential bound from Hoeffding (when t ≫ σ²/M).

8.5.3 McDiarmid’s Inequality

McDiarmid’s inequality extends concentration results to functions of independent random variables with bounded differences.

Theorem 9.7 (McDiarmid’s Inequality). Let X₁, …, Xₙ be independent random variables and let f: 𝒳₁ × ⋯ × 𝒳ₙ → ℝ satisfy the bounded difference condition:

|f(x₁, …, xᵢ, …, xₙ) - f(x₁, …, x’ᵢ, …, xₙ)| ≤ cᵢ

for all i and all xᵢ, x’ᵢ ∈ 𝒳ᵢ. Let Z = f(X₁, …, Xₙ) and μ = E[Z]. Then

P(Z - μ ≥ t) ≤ exp(-2t²/∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ cᵢ²)

for all t ≥ 0.

McDiarmid’s inequality is particularly useful in algorithmic applications where we analyze functions of random inputs, such as empirical risk in learning theory.

8.5.4 Example 9.4: Application to Empirical Processes

Consider the empirical distribution function Fₙ(x) = (1/n)∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ 𝟙{Xᵢ ≤ x} for i.i.d. samples X₁, …, Xₙ from distribution F. The Kolmogorov-Smirnov statistic Dₙ = sup_x |Fₙ(x) - F(x)| measures the maximum deviation between empirical and true distribution functions.

Using McDiarmid’s inequality with cᵢ = 1/n (since changing one observation can change Dₙ by at most 1/n), we obtain:

P(Dₙ - E[Dₙ] ≥ t) ≤ exp(-2nt²)

using Distributions, Random
Random.seed!(42)

function kolmogorov_smirnov_simulation(n_samples, n_trials=1000)
    """Simulate KS statistic and verify concentration bound"""
    true_dist = Normal(0, 1)
    ks_statistics = Float64[]
    
    for trial in 1:n_trials
        # Generate sample
        samples = rand(true_dist, n_samples)
        
        # Compute empirical distribution function at grid points
        x_grid = -3:0.1:3
        empirical_cdf = [mean(samples .<= x) for x in x_grid]
        true_cdf = [cdf(true_dist, x) for x in x_grid]
        
        # Compute KS statistic
        ks_stat = maximum(abs.(empirical_cdf .- true_cdf))
        push!(ks_statistics, ks_stat)
    end
    
    return ks_statistics
end

# Compare empirical distribution to McDiarmid bound
n = 100
ks_stats = kolmogorov_smirnov_simulation(n)
mean_ks = mean(ks_stats)

# Theoretical bound: P(D_n - E[D_n] ≥ t) ≤ exp(-2nt²)
t_values = 0.01:0.01:0.15
empirical_tails = [mean(ks_stats .- mean_ks .≥ t) for t in t_values]
mcdiarimid_bounds = [exp(-2 * n * t^2) for t in t_values]

plot(t_values, empirical_tails, label="Empirical P(D_n - E[D_n] ≥ t)", 
     marker=:circle, markersize=3)
plot!(t_values, mcdiarimid_bounds, label="McDiarmid bound: e^{-2nt²}", 
      line=:dash, linewidth=2)
xlabel!("t")
ylabel!("Probability")
title!("Concentration of Kolmogorov-Smirnov Statistic (n=$n)")
yscale!(:log10)

8.6 9.6 Sub-Exponential Random Variables

Sub-exponential random variables extend the sub-Gaussian class to include heavier-tailed distributions while maintaining exponential concentration properties.

Definition 9.5 (Sub-Exponential Random Variable). A random variable X with E[X] = 0 is sub-exponential with parameters (ν, α) if

E[e^{tX}] ≤ e^{ν²t²/2}

for all |t| ≤ 1/α.

The parameter ν controls the “Gaussian” regime where the MGF behaves quadratically, while α determines the threshold beyond which exponential growth dominates.

Theorem 9.8 (Sub-Exponential Tail Bound). If X is sub-exponential with parameters (ν, α), then

P(|X| ≥ t) ≤ 2exp(-min{t²/(2ν²), t/(2α)})

for all t ≥ 0.

This bound exhibits a phase transition: Gaussian-type decay exp(-t²/(2ν²)) for small t, transitioning to exponential decay exp(-t/(2α)) for large t.

8.6.1 Examples of Sub-Exponential Random Variables

  1. Chi-squared variables: If Z ~ N(0,1), then Z² - 1 is sub-exponential.
  2. Exponential variables: If X ~ Exp(1), then X - 1 is sub-exponential.
  3. Bounded variables: Any bounded random variable is sub-exponential (and sub-Gaussian).

8.7 9.7 Applications to High-Dimensional Statistics

Concentration inequalities are fundamental tools in modern high-dimensional statistics, providing theoretical guarantees for statistical procedures when the dimension p may be comparable to or larger than the sample size n.

8.7.1 Matrix Concentration

Consider a random matrix S = (1/n)∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ XᵢXᵢᵀ where X₁, …, Xₙ are independent random vectors in ℝᵖ. Matrix concentration inequalities bound the spectral norm ‖S - E[S]‖ (largest singular value).

Theorem 9.9 (Matrix Bernstein Inequality). Let Z₁, …, Zₙ be independent random matrices in ℝᵖˣᵖ with E[Zᵢ] = 0, ‖Zᵢ‖ ≤ R almost surely, and variance proxy σ² = ‖∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ E[ZᵢZᵢᵀ]‖. Then for the sum S = ∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ Zᵢ,

P(‖S‖ ≥ t) ≤ 2p exp(-t²/(2σ² + 2Rt/3))

8.7.2 Application to Covariance Estimation

Let X₁, …, Xₙ be i.i.d. sub-Gaussian vectors in ℝᵖ with covariance Σ. The sample covariance matrix Ŝ = (1/n)∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ (Xᵢ - X̄)(Xᵢ - X̄)ᵀ satisfies, under appropriate conditions:

P(‖Ŝ - Σ‖ ≥ t) ≤ 2p exp(-cnt²)

for some constant c > 0. This bound is meaningful when n ≳ p log p, showing that accurate covariance estimation is possible in moderately high dimensions.

using LinearAlgebra, Distributions, Random
Random.seed!(123)

function covariance_concentration_experiment(p, n, n_trials=200)
    """Empirically verify matrix concentration for sample covariance"""
    spectral_errors = Float64[]
    
    # True covariance is identity
    Σ_true = Matrix(I, p, p)
    
    for trial in 1:n_trials
        # Generate n samples from p-dimensional standard normal
        X = randn(n, p)
        X_centered = X .- mean(X, dims=1)
        
        # Compute sample covariance
        Σ_hat = (X_centered' * X_centered) / (n - 1)
        
        # Compute spectral norm of error
        error_norm = opnorm(Σ_hat - Σ_true, 2)
        push!(spectral_errors, error_norm)
    end
    
    return spectral_errors
end

# Test for different problem dimensions
dimensions = [10, 25, 50]
sample_sizes = [100, 200, 400]

for (p, n) in zip(dimensions, sample_sizes)
    errors = covariance_concentration_experiment(p, n)
    println("p=$p, n=$n: Mean error = $(round(mean(errors), digits=4)), " *
           "95th percentile = $(round(quantile(errors, 0.95), digits=4))")
end

# Visualize concentration for one case
p, n = 25, 200
errors = covariance_concentration_experiment(p, n, 500)

histogram(errors, bins=20, normalize=:pdf, alpha=0.7, 
         label="Empirical distribution")
xlabel!("||Ŝ - Σ||₂")
ylabel!("Density")
title!("Spectral Error Distribution: Sample Covariance (p=$p, n=$n)")

8.8 9.8 Applications to Learning Theory

Concentration inequalities provide the foundation for generalization bounds in statistical learning theory, quantifying how well empirical risk approximates population risk.

8.8.1 Rademacher Complexity and Generalization

Let 𝒮 = {(X₁, Y₁), …, (Xₙ, Yₙ)} be a training sample and ℱ be a function class. The empirical Rademacher complexity is defined as:

R̂ₙ(ℱ) = E_ε[sup_{f∈ℱ} (1/n)|∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ εᵢf(Xᵢ)|]

where ε₁, …, εₙ are independent Rademacher variables (±1 with equal probability).

Theorem 9.10 (Rademacher Generalization Bound). With probability at least 1-δ, for all f ∈ ℱ,

|R(f) - R̂(f)| ≤ 2R̂ₙ(ℱ) + √(log(2/δ)/(2n))

where R(f) is the population risk and R̂(f) is the empirical risk.

This bound combines the complexity of the function class (measured by Rademacher complexity) with concentration of measure to provide generalization guarantees.

8.8.2 Example 9.5: Linear Predictors

For the class of linear predictors ℱ = {x ↦ ⟨w, x⟩ : ‖w‖₂ ≤ W} with inputs ‖x‖₂ ≤ X, the Rademacher complexity satisfies:

R̂ₙ(ℱ) ≤ WX/√n

This gives a generalization bound of O(WX/√n), showing that linear predictors generalize well when the norm constraints W and X are moderate.

8.9 9.9 Exercises

Exercise 9.1 (Computational). For X ~ Binomial(n, p), derive the probability generating function and use it to compute the mean and variance. Implement both the exact PGF formula and a numerical verification in Julia for n = 20, p = 0.3.

Exercise 9.2 (Theoretical). Prove that if X and Y are independent random variables with MGFs M_X(t) and M_Y(t), then the MGF of X + Y is M_{X+Y}(t) = M_X(t)M_Y(t). Use this to derive the MGF of a sum of independent exponential random variables.

Exercise 9.3 (Computational). Implement a function to compute the Chernoff bound for a sum of independent Bernoulli(p) random variables. Compare this bound with Hoeffding’s inequality and the exact binomial tail probabilities for various parameter choices.

Exercise 9.4 (Theoretical). Show that if X is sub-Gaussian with parameter σ, then aX is sub-Gaussian with parameter |a|σ for any constant a. Prove that the sum of independent sub-Gaussian random variables is sub-Gaussian.

Exercise 9.5 (Applications). Consider the empirical risk R̂(f) = (1/n)∑ᵢ₌₁ⁿ ℓ(f(xᵢ), yᵢ) where ℓ is the squared loss bounded by M. Use McDiarmid’s inequality to derive a concentration bound for |R̂(f) - R(f)| and implement a simulation to verify the bound.

Exercise 9.6 (Theoretical). Prove Bernstein’s inequality by establishing the MGF bound E[e^{tX}] ≤ exp(t²σ²/2 · h(tM/σ²)) where h(u) = (e^u - 1 - u)/u² for a zero-mean random variable X with |X| ≤ M and Var(X) ≤ σ².

Exercise 9.7 (Computational). Implement a simulation study comparing the empirical tail behavior of various sub-exponential random variables (e.g., χ² - 1, Exp(1) - 1, t-distribution with ν degrees of freedom) to their theoretical sub-exponential bounds.

**Exercise 9

9 Chapter 10: Kolmogorov Extension and Infinite-Dimensional Probability

In our journey through measure-theoretic probability, we have thus far worked primarily with finite-dimensional probability spaces. However, many fundamental objects in probability theory—stochastic processes, random fields, and infinite sequences of random variables—naturally live in infinite-dimensional spaces. The construction of probability measures on such spaces presents profound technical challenges that cannot be addressed by simple extensions of finite-dimensional techniques.

This chapter establishes the theoretical foundation for infinite-dimensional probability through Kolmogorov’s extension theorem, one of the most elegant and powerful results in modern probability theory. We develop the machinery of consistent families of finite-dimensional distributions and show how they uniquely determine probability measures on infinite product spaces. The culmination of our development is the rigorous construction of Brownian motion and other Gaussian processes, providing concrete realizations of these fundamental stochastic objects.

9.1 Consistent Families and Finite-Dimensional Distributions

The key insight underlying infinite-dimensional probability is that any probability measure on an infinite product space is completely determined by its finite-dimensional marginal distributions, provided these marginals satisfy natural consistency conditions.

9.1.1 Product Spaces and Cylinder Sets

Let \((E, \mathcal{E})\) be a measurable space, and consider the infinite product space \(E^{\mathbb{N}} = \prod_{n=1}^{\infty} E\). Each element \(\omega \in E^{\mathbb{N}}\) is an infinite sequence \(\omega = (\omega_1, \omega_2, \ldots)\) with \(\omega_n \in E\) for each \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).

For any finite subset \(F \subset \mathbb{N}\) with \(|F| = k\), we can define the canonical projection \(\pi_F: E^{\mathbb{N}} \to E^k\) by \[\pi_F(\omega) = (\omega_{i_1}, \omega_{i_2}, \ldots, \omega_{i_k})\] where \(F = \{i_1, i_2, \ldots, i_k\}\) with \(i_1 < i_2 < \ldots < i_k\).

Definition 10.1 (Cylinder Sets). Let \(F \subset \mathbb{N}\) be finite and \(A \in \mathcal{E}^{|F|}\). The cylinder set with base \(A\) and index set \(F\) is \[C_{F,A} = \{\omega \in E^{\mathbb{N}} : \pi_F(\omega) \in A\} = \pi_F^{-1}(A).\]

The collection of all cylinder sets forms an algebra (but not a σ-algebra) on \(E^{\mathbb{N}}\). The product σ-algebra \(\mathcal{E}^{\mathbb{N}}\) is defined as the σ-algebra generated by all cylinder sets.

Definition 10.2 (Product σ-Algebra). The product σ-algebra on \(E^{\mathbb{N}}\) is \[\mathcal{E}^{\mathbb{N}} = \sigma\left(\bigcup_{F \subset \mathbb{N}, |F| < \infty} \pi_F^{-1}(\mathcal{E}^{|F|})\right).\]

9.1.2 Consistent Families of Measures

The central concept in Kolmogorov’s theory is that of a consistent family of finite-dimensional distributions.

Definition 10.3 (Consistent Family). A family \(\{\mu_F : F \subset \mathbb{N}, |F| < \infty\}\) of probability measures, where \(\mu_F\) is a probability measure on \((E^{|F|}, \mathcal{E}^{|F|})\), is called consistent if for any finite sets \(F \subset G \subset \mathbb{N}\) and any \(A \in \mathcal{E}^{|F|}\), \[\mu_F(A) = \mu_G(\pi_{G \to F}^{-1}(A)),\] where \(\pi_{G \to F}: E^{|G|} \to E^{|F|}\) is the natural projection obtained by removing coordinates not in \(F\).

The consistency condition ensures that marginal distributions agree when computed from different finite-dimensional projections. This is precisely the condition needed to guarantee the existence of a unique probability measure on the infinite product space.

9.1.3 The Kolmogorov Extension Theorem

The fundamental theorem of this chapter establishes that consistent families uniquely extend to probability measures on infinite product spaces.

Theorem 10.1 (Kolmogorov Extension Theorem). Let \((E, \mathcal{E})\) be a measurable space, and let \(\{\mu_F : F \subset \mathbb{N}, |F| < \infty\}\) be a consistent family of probability measures. Then there exists a unique probability measure \(\mathbb{P}\) on \((E^{\mathbb{N}}, \mathcal{E}^{\mathbb{N}})\) such that for every finite \(F \subset \mathbb{N}\) and \(A \in \mathcal{E}^{|F|}\), \[\mathbb{P}(\pi_F^{-1}(A)) = \mu_F(A).\]

Proof. The proof proceeds through several steps, utilizing the extension of measures from algebras to σ-algebras.

Step 1: Define \(\mathbb{P}\) on cylinder sets. For any cylinder set \(C_{F,A} = \pi_F^{-1}(A)\), define \(\mathbb{P}(C_{F,A}) = \mu_F(A)\). The consistency condition ensures this definition is unambiguous: if \(C_{F,A} = C_{G,B}\) for some other finite set \(G\) and \(B \in \mathcal{E}^{|G|}\), then \(\mu_F(A) = \mu_G(B)\).

Step 2: Show \(\mathbb{P}\) is finitely additive on the cylinder algebra. Let \(\mathcal{C}\) denote the algebra of cylinder sets. For disjoint cylinder sets \(C_1, \ldots, C_n \in \mathcal{C}\), we can find a common finite index set \(F\) such that each \(C_i = \pi_F^{-1}(A_i)\) for disjoint sets \(A_i \in \mathcal{E}^{|F|}\). Then \[\mathbb{P}\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^n C_i\right) = \mathbb{P}\left(\pi_F^{-1}\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^n A_i\right)\right) = \mu_F\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^n A_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^n \mu_F(A_i) = \sum_{i=1}^n \mathbb{P}(C_i).\]

Step 3: Establish σ-additivity on cylinders. The key technical challenge is proving that \(\mathbb{P}\) is σ-additive on \(\mathcal{C}\). This requires showing that if \((C_n)\) is a decreasing sequence of cylinder sets with \(\bigcap_n C_n = \emptyset\), then \(\lim_n \mathbb{P}(C_n) = 0\).

By the finite intersection property and compactness arguments in the product topology (when \(E\) is equipped with a suitable topology), this follows from the fact that each \(\mu_F\) is a probability measure.

Step 4: Extend to the σ-algebra. By the Carathéodory extension theorem (Chapter 1), \(\mathbb{P}\) extends uniquely to a probability measure on \(\mathcal{E}^{\mathbb{N}} = \sigma(\mathcal{C})\).

Step 5: Uniqueness. Any probability measure on \((E^{\mathbb{N}}, \mathcal{E}^{\mathbb{N}})\) is uniquely determined by its values on cylinder sets, establishing uniqueness. □

The Kolmogorov extension theorem is fundamental because it reduces the problem of constructing infinite-dimensional probability measures to the simpler task of specifying consistent finite-dimensional distributions.

9.2 Construction of Stochastic Processes

The Kolmogorov extension theorem provides the theoretical foundation for rigorously defining stochastic processes as measurable functions on probability spaces.

Definition 10.4 (Stochastic Process). Let \((E, \mathcal{E})\) be a measurable space and \(T\) be an index set. A stochastic process \((X_t)_{t \in T}\) is a collection of \(E\)-valued random variables defined on a common probability space \((\Omega, \mathcal{F}, \mathbb{P})\).

When \(T\) is countable, we can identify the process with a single random element \(X: \Omega \to E^T\) defined by \(X(\omega) = (X_t(\omega))_{t \in T}\). The distribution of this random element is called the process distribution.

9.2.1 Canonical Construction

Given a consistent family of finite-dimensional distributions \(\{\mu_F\}\), we can construct a stochastic process canonically on the path space.

Definition 10.5 (Canonical Process). Let \(\mathbb{P}\) be the probability measure on \((E^{\mathbb{N}}, \mathcal{E}^{\mathbb{N}})\) given by Kolmogorov extension from a consistent family \(\{\mu_F\}\). The canonical process \((X_n)_{n=1}^{\infty}\) on \((\mathbb{E}^{\mathbb{N}}, \mathcal{E}^{\mathbb{N}}, \mathbb{P})\) is defined by \[X_n(\omega) = \omega_n\] for \(\omega = (\omega_1, \omega_2, \ldots) \in E^{\mathbb{N}}\).

The canonical process has the property that its finite-dimensional distributions exactly match the given consistent family: for any finite \(F = \{n_1, \ldots, n_k\} \subset \mathbb{N}\) and \(A \in \mathcal{E}^k\), \[\mathbb{P}((X_{n_1}, \ldots, X_{n_k}) \in A) = \mu_F(A).\]

using Distributions, Random, LinearAlgebra, Plots

# Demonstration: Construction of a simple Markov chain
# via consistent finite-dimensional distributions

"""
Construct finite-dimensional distributions for a simple random walk
"""
function random_walk_fdds(n::Int, p::Float64 = 0.5)
    # For a simple random walk, X_n = X_{n-1} + Z_n where Z_n ~ Bernoulli(p) - 0.5
    # Starting from X_0 = 0
    
    # The n-dimensional distribution is determined by the transition probabilities
    function sample_path(rng, n)
        path = zeros(Int, n)
        for i in 2:n
            step = rand(rng) < p ? 1 : -1
            path[i] = path[i-1] + step
        end
        return path
    end
    
    return sample_path
end

# Generate sample paths
Random.seed!(123)
rng = MersenneTwister(123)
sampler = random_walk_fdds(100, 0.6)

# Generate multiple sample paths
n_paths = 5
paths = [sampler(rng, 100) for _ in 1:n_paths]

# Plot the paths
p = plot(title="Sample Paths of Simple Random Walk", 
         xlabel="Time", ylabel="Position",
         legend=:topleft, size=(800, 500))

for (i, path) in enumerate(paths)
    plot!(p, 1:length(path), path, label="Path $i", alpha=0.7, linewidth=2)
end

display(p)

# Verify consistency: marginal at time n should match 
# the marginal distribution computed from longer paths
println("Consistency check:")
n_test = 50
short_samples = [sampler(rng, n_test)[end] for _ in 1:10000]
long_samples = [sampler(rng, 100)[n_test] for _ in 1:10000]

println("Mean of X_$n_test from $n_test-dimensional samples: $(mean(short_samples))")
println("Mean of X_$n_test from 100-dimensional samples: $(mean(long_samples))")
println("Variance of X_$n_test from $n_test-dimensional samples: $(var(short_samples))")
println("Variance of X_$n_test from 100-dimensional samples: $(var(long_samples))")

9.3 Gaussian Processes and Wiener Measure

One of the most important applications of the Kolmogorov extension theorem is the rigorous construction of Gaussian processes, particularly Brownian motion.

9.3.1 Finite-Dimensional Gaussian Distributions

Definition 10.6 (Gaussian Process). A stochastic process \((X_t)_{t \in T}\) is called a Gaussian process if for any finite collection \(t_1, \ldots, t_n \in T\), the random vector \((X_{t_1}, \ldots, X_{t_n})\) has a multivariate normal distribution.

A Gaussian process is completely characterized by its mean function \(m(t) = \mathbb{E}[X_t]\) and covariance function \(K(s,t) = \text{Cov}(X_s, X_t)\).

Theorem 10.2 (Existence of Gaussian Processes). Let \(T\) be any index set, \(m: T \to \mathbb{R}\) be any function, and \(K: T \times T \to \mathbb{R}\) be any positive semidefinite kernel (i.e., for any finite collection \(t_1, \ldots, t_n \in T\), the matrix \((K(t_i, t_j))_{i,j}\) is positive semidefinite). Then there exists a Gaussian process \((X_t)_{t \in T}\) with mean function \(m\) and covariance function \(K\).

Proof. For any finite subset \(F = \{t_1, \ldots, t_n\} \subset T\), define \(\mu_F\) to be the \(n\)-dimensional normal distribution with mean vector \((m(t_1), \ldots, m(t_n))\) and covariance matrix \((K(t_i, t_j))_{i,j}\).

The consistency of this family follows from properties of multivariate normal distributions: marginals of multivariate normal distributions are multivariate normal with the appropriate mean and covariance parameters.

By the Kolmogorov extension theorem, these consistent finite-dimensional distributions determine a unique probability measure on \((\mathbb{R}^T, \mathcal{B}(\mathbb{R}^T))\), yielding the desired Gaussian process. □

9.3.2 Brownian Motion via Kolmogorov Extension

Definition 10.7 (Brownian Motion). A stochastic process \((B_t)_{t \geq 0}\) is called Brownian motion (or a Wiener process) if: 1. \(B_0 = 0\) almost surely 2. For any \(0 \leq t_1 < t_2 < \ldots < t_n\), the increments \(B_{t_2} - B_{t_1}, B_{t_3} - B_{t_2}, \ldots, B_{t_n} - B_{t_{n-1}}\) are independent 3. For any \(s < t\), \(B_t - B_s \sim \mathcal{N}(0, t-s)\) 4. The paths \(t \mapsto B_t(\omega)\) are continuous almost surely

The existence of Brownian motion with continuous paths requires more advanced techniques beyond the scope of this chapter. However, we can easily construct the finite-dimensional distributions.

Theorem 10.3 (Finite-Dimensional Distributions of Brownian Motion). The finite-dimensional distributions of Brownian motion are given by: for \(0 \leq t_1 < t_2 < \ldots < t_n\), the vector \((B_{t_1}, \ldots, B_{t_n})\) has a multivariate normal distribution with mean zero and covariance matrix \(\Sigma\) where \(\Sigma_{ij} = \min(t_i, t_j)\).

Proof. This follows from the independence and normality of increments. For \(i \leq j\), \[\text{Cov}(B_{t_i}, B_{t_j}) = \text{Cov}(B_{t_i}, B_{t_i} + (B_{t_j} - B_{t_i})) = \text{Var}(B_{t_i}) = t_i = \min(t_i, t_j).\]

The case \(i > j\) follows by symmetry. □

using Distributions, LinearAlgebra, Random, Plots

"""
Generate finite-dimensional distributions for Brownian motion
"""
function brownian_motion_fdd(times::Vector{Float64})
    n = length(times)
    @assert issorted(times) "Times must be sorted"
    @assert times[1] >= 0 "Times must be non-negative"
    
    # Covariance matrix: Σ_ij = min(t_i, t_j)
    Σ = [min(times[i], times[j]) for i in 1:n, j in 1:n]
    
    return MvNormal(zeros(n), Σ)
end

# Example: Sample paths of Brownian motion
times = collect(0.0:0.01:1.0)
n_times = length(times)

# Generate the finite-dimensional distribution
bm_dist = brownian_motion_fdd(times)

# Sample multiple paths
Random.seed!(42)
n_paths = 5
paths = [rand(bm_dist) for _ in 1:n_paths]

# Plot the sample paths
p = plot(title="Sample Paths of Brownian Motion", 
         xlabel="Time", ylabel="B(t)",
         legend=:bottomright, size=(800, 500))

for (i, path) in enumerate(paths)
    plot!(p, times, path, label="Path $i", alpha=0.7, linewidth=2)
end

display(p)

# Verify theoretical properties
println("Verification of Brownian motion properties:")

# Property: E[B_t] = 0
t_test = 0.5
samples_at_t = [rand(brownian_motion_fdd([t_test]))[1] for _ in 1:10000]
println("E[B($(t_test))] ≈ $(mean(samples_at_t)) (should be ≈ 0)")
println("Var[B($(t_test))] ≈ $(var(samples_at_t)) (should be ≈ $(t_test))")

# Property: Covariance structure
s, t = 0.3, 0.7
samples_st = [rand(brownian_motion_fdd([s, t])) for _ in 1:10000]
cov_empirical = cov([sample[1] for sample in samples_st], 
                    [sample[2] for sample in samples_st])
cov_theoretical = min(s, t)
println("Cov[B($s), B($t)] ≈ $(cov_empirical) (should be ≈ $(cov_theoretical))")

# Property: Independence of increments
# B(t2) - B(t1) and B(t4) - B(t3) should be independent for non-overlapping intervals
t1, t2, t3, t4 = 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8
samples_4d = [rand(brownian_motion_fdd([t1, t2, t3, t4])) for _ in 1:10000]
incr1 = [sample[2] - sample[1] for sample in samples_4d]  # B(t2) - B(t1)
incr2 = [sample[4] - sample[3] for sample in samples_4d]  # B(t4) - B(t3)
cov_increments = cov(incr1, incr2)
println("Cov[B($t2)-B($t1), B($t4)-B($t3)] ≈ $(cov_increments) (should be ≈ 0)")

9.3.3 The Wiener Measure

The probability measure on the space of continuous functions \(C([0,1], \mathbb{R})\) induced by Brownian motion is called Wiener measure.

Definition 10.8 (Wiener Measure). Let \(C([0,1], \mathbb{R})\) denote the space of continuous functions from \([0,1]\) to \(\mathbb{R}\) equipped with the Borel σ-algebra induced by the supremum norm \(\|f\|_\infty = \sup_{t \in [0,1]} |f(t)|\). The Wiener measure \(W\) is the unique probability measure on \(C([0,1], \mathbb{R})\) such that the coordinate process \(B_t(f) = f(t)\) is Brownian motion.

The construction of Wiener measure requires sophisticated techniques from functional analysis, as the space \(C([0,1], \mathbb{R})\) is not a finite product space. However, the finite-dimensional distributions are completely determined by Theorem 10.3.

9.4 Advanced Topics and Extensions

9.4.1 Tightness and Prokhorov’s Theorem

In many applications, we need to construct probability measures on spaces of functions with specific regularity properties. The theory of tightness provides powerful tools for this purpose.

Definition 10.9 (Tightness). A collection \(\mathcal{P}\) of probability measures on a metric space \((S, d)\) is called tight if for every \(\epsilon > 0\), there exists a compact set \(K \subset S\) such that \(\mathbb{P}(K) \geq 1 - \epsilon\) for all \(\mathbb{P} \in \mathcal{P}\).

Theorem 10.4 (Prokhorov’s Theorem). Let \((S, d)\) be a complete separable metric space. A collection \(\mathcal{P}\) of probability measures on \(S\) is tight if and only if every sequence in \(\mathcal{P}\) contains a weakly convergent subsequence.

This theorem is fundamental in establishing the existence of measures on function spaces and proving convergence of stochastic processes.

9.4.2 Kolmogorov’s Continuity Criterion

A crucial question for stochastic processes is when sample paths have desired regularity properties.

Theorem 10.5 (Kolmogorov’s Continuity Criterion). Let \((X_t)_{t \in [0,1]}\) be a stochastic process such that for some constants \(\alpha > 0\), \(\beta > 0\), and \(C > 0\), \[\mathbb{E}[|X_t - X_s|^\alpha] \leq C|t - s|^{1+\beta}\] for all \(s, t \in [0,1]\). Then \(X\) has a continuous modification, and for any \(\gamma < \beta/\alpha\), the paths are Hölder continuous of order \(\gamma\) almost surely.

This criterion is essential for proving that Brownian motion has continuous sample paths.

using Random, Statistics, LinearAlgebra

"""
Demonstrate Kolmogorov's continuity criterion
by checking Hölder continuity for approximate Brownian motion
"""
function check_holder_continuity(path::Vector{Float64}, times::Vector{Float64}, γ::Float64)
    n = length(path)
    @assert length(times) == n
    
    max_ratio = 0.0
    for i in 1:(n-1)
        for j in (i+1):n
            if times[j] - times[i] > 0
                ratio = abs(path[j] - path[i]) / (times[j] - times[i])^γ
                max_ratio = max(max_ratio, ratio)
            end
        end
    end
    return max_ratio
end

# Generate fine-grid Brownian motion approximation
Random.seed!(123)
n_points = 1001
times = collect(LinRange(0.0, 1.0, n_points))
dt = times[2] - times[1]

# Generate using increments (more accurate than fdd for fine grids)
path = zeros(n_points)
for i in 2:n_points
    path[i] = path[i-1] + sqrt(dt) * randn()
end

println("Testing Hölder continuity for Brownian motion approximation:")
println("Grid spacing: $dt")

# Test different Hölder exponents
gammas = [0.3, 0.4, 0.5, 0.6]
for γ in gammas
    max_ratio = check_holder_continuity(path, times, γ)
    println("γ = $γ: max |B(t) - B(s)|/|t - s|^γ = $(round(max_ratio, digits=3))")
end

println("\nTheory predicts: Brownian motion is γ-Hölder continuous for any γ < 1/2")
println("but not for γ ≥ 1/2. The ratios should be bounded for γ < 1/2.")

9.5 Connections to Functional Analysis

The construction of infinite-dimensional probability measures connects deeply with functional analysis, particularly the theory of measures on Banach spaces.

9.5.1 Gaussian Measures on Banach Spaces

The theory extends to Gaussian measures on infinite-dimensional Banach spaces, where the Kolmogorov extension approach must be supplemented with tools from functional analysis.

Definition 10.10 (Gaussian Measure on Banach Space). Let \(B\) be a separable Banach space. A probability measure \(\mu\) on \((B, \mathcal{B}(B))\) is called Gaussian if for every continuous linear functional \(f \in B^*\), the random variable \(f(X)\) is normally distributed when \(X \sim \mu\).

The existence and characterization of Gaussian measures on Banach spaces requires the theory of reproducing kernel Hilbert spaces and covariance operators.

9.5.2 Cameron-Martin Space

For Wiener measure, the Cameron-Martin space characterizes the directions of absolute continuity.

Definition 10.11 (Cameron-Martin Space). The Cameron-Martin space \(H\) associated with Wiener measure is the Hilbert space of absolutely continuous functions \(h: [0,1] \to \mathbb{R}\) with \(h(0) = 0\) and \(\dot{h} \in L^2([0,1])\), equipped with the inner product \[\langle h_1, h_2 \rangle_H = \int_0^1 \dot{h}_1(t) \dot{h}_2(t) dt.\]

The Cameron-Martin theorem characterizes when translation by a deterministic function preserves absolute continuity with respect to Wiener measure.

9.6 Exercises

  1. Consistency Verification: Let \(\{X_n\}_{n=1}^\infty\) be independent random variables with \(X_n \sim \mathcal{N}(0, 1/n)\).
    1. Define the finite-dimensional distributions \(\mu_F\) for finite sets \(F \subset \mathbb{N}\).
    2. Verify that this family is consistent.
    3. Implement in Julia and verify consistency numerically for several examples.
  2. Markov Chain Construction: Consider a two-state Markov chain with transition matrix \(P = \begin{pmatrix} 1-p & p \\ q & 1-q \end{pmatrix}\) where \(p, q \in (0,1)\).
    1. Construct the consistent family of finite-dimensional distributions starting from the stationary distribution.
    2. Prove that your family satisfies the consistency condition.
    3. Implement the construction in Julia and generate sample paths.
  3. Ornstein-Uhlenbeck Process: The Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process \((X_t)_{t \geq 0}\) is the unique Gaussian process with \(X_0 = 0\), \(\mathbb{E}[X_t] = 0\), and \(\text{Cov}(X_s, X_t) = \frac{1}{2\theta}(e^{-\theta|t-s|} - e^{-\theta(t+s)})\) for \(\theta > 0\).
    1. Verify that this covariance function is positive semidefinite.
    2. Construct the finite-dimensional distributions.
    3. Implement in Julia and generate sample paths. Compare with Brownian motion.
  4. Brownian Bridge: The Brownian bridge is the Gaussian process obtained by conditioning Brownian motion to satisfy \(B_1 = 0\).
    1. Show that the covariance function is \(K(s,t) = \min(s,t) - st\) for \(s, t \in [0,1]\).
    2. Prove that this defines a consistent family.
    3. Implement the construction and verify that sample paths satisfy the boundary conditions.
  5. Hölder Continuity:
    1. Prove that if \((X_t)_{t \in [0,1]}\) satisfies \(\mathbb{E}[|X_t - X_s|^4] \leq C|t - s|^2\) for some constant \(C\), then \(X\) has a modification that is \(\gamma\)-Hölder continuous for any \(\gamma < 1/4\).
    2. Verify this computationally for Brownian motion using the bound \(\mathbb{E}[|B_t - B_s|^4] = 3|t - s|^2\).
  6. Non-Gaussian Example: Consider the family of finite-dimensional distributions where each \((X_{t_1}, \ldots, X_{t_n})\) has independent components with \(X_{t_i} \sim \text{Exp}(t_i)\) (exponential with rate \(t_i > 0\)).
    1. Show that this family is not consistent.
    2. Modify the construction to obtain a consistent family.
    3. Implement both versions in Julia and demonstrate the difference.
  7. Cylindrical Measures:
    1. Give an example of a finitely additive measure on the cylinder algebra that does not extend to a σ-additive measure on the product σ-algebra.
    2. Explain why the consistency condition is necessary for Kolmogorov extension.
  8. Functional Central Limit Theorem: Let \(S_n(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \sum_{i=1}^{\lfloor nt \rfloor} X_i\) where \(X_i\) are i.i.d. with mean 0 and variance 1.
    1. Show that the finite-dimensional distributions of \(S_n\) converge to those of Brownian motion.
    2. Implement this convergence in Julia, plotting sample paths for increasing values of \(n\).
  9. Two-Parameter Processes: Extend the Kolmogorov construction to processes indexed by \(\mathbb{R}_+^2\).
    1. Define Brownian sheet as the Gaussian process with covariance \(K((s_1,t_1), (s_2,t_2)) = \min(s_1,s_2)\min(t_1,t_2)\).
    2. Implement the finite-dimensional distributions and generate sample “surfaces”.
  10. Measure Equivalence: Two probability measures \(\mathbb{P}\) and \(\mathbb{Q}\) on \((E^{\mathbb{N}}, \mathcal{E}^{\mathbb{N}})\) are equivalent if they have the same null sets.
    1. Prove that \(\mathbb{P} \sim \mathbb{Q}\) if and only if their finite-dimensional distributions are mutually absolutely continuous.
    2. Apply this to compare different Gaussian processes.

9.7 References

Billingsley, P. (1995). Probability and Measure. 3rd edition, John Wiley & Sons.

Dudley, R.M. (2002). Real Analysis and Probability. Cambridge University Press.

Kallenberg, O. (2002). Foundations of Modern Probability. 2nd edition, Springer-Verlag.

Kolmogorov, A.N. (1933). Grundbegriffe der Wahrscheinlichkeitsrechnung. Springer-Verlag
